source_paper_title: Estimating a Function and Its Derivatives Under a Smoothness Condition
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Lim_2025, title={Estimating a Function and Its Derivatives Under a Smoothness Condition}, volume={50}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2020.0161}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2020.0161}, number={2}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Lim, Eunji}, year={2025}, month=may, pages={1112–1138} }
context:
Section 7.3 (page 15 of 27 in the provided text) and Remark 4 (page 10 of 27)

We conjecture that the conclusions of Corollary 2 hold under a more natural condition that \(S_n \to \sigma^2\) as \(n \to \infty\). This remains as an open question. Another open question is computing the convergence rate of \(\hat g_n\) under suitable conditions on \(S_n\).
title: Prove consistency and rates for smoothness-minimizing estimator under variance target
background_latex:
Let \(d\ge 1\) and \([a,b]^d\subset \mathbb{R}^d\) be a compact hyperrectangle. Let \(m\in\mathbb{N}\) satisfy \(2m>d\). Let \(\alpha=(\alpha_1,\dots,\alpha_d)\in\mathbb{Z}_+^d\) be a multi-index with order \(|\alpha|=\sum_{j=1}^d \alpha_j\), and let \(D^\alpha\) denote the (weak) partial derivative operator.

Let \(\mathcal{F}_m\) be the space of generalized functions (Schwartz distributions) on \(\mathbb{R}^d\) whose weak partial derivatives of order \(m\) are square-integrable, i.e.
\[\mathcal{F}_m:=\Bigl\{f:\mathbb{R}^d\to\mathbb{R}: \int_{\mathbb{R}^d} (D^\alpha f(x))^2\,dx<\infty\ \text{for all }\alpha\text{ with }|\alpha|=m\Bigr\}.\]
Define the smoothness (roughness) functional
\[J(f):=\sum_{|\alpha|=m}\int_{\mathbb{R}^d} (D^\alpha f(x))^2\,dx,\]
and the empirical squared error for data \((X_i,Y_i)_{i=1}^n\) by
\[E_n(f):=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n (Y_i-f(X_i))^2.\]
Assume observations satisfy the regression model \(Y_i=f^*(X_i)+\varepsilon_i\), where \((X_i,\varepsilon_i)\) are i.i.d., \(X_i\in[a,b]^d\) has a positive continuous density on \([a,b]^d\), \(\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon_i\mid X_i]=0\), and \(\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon_i^2\mid X_i]=\sigma^2<\infty\). Let \(f^*\in\mathcal{F}_m\).

Consider the smoothness-minimizing estimator \(\hat g_n\) defined (for a given tolerance \(S_n\ge 0\)) as any solution of
\[\min_{f\in\mathcal{F}_m} J(f)\quad\text{subject to}\quad E_n(f)\le S_n.\]
This is Problem (B) in the paper. The paper notes that if an additional inequality holds,
\[\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n (\hat g_n(X_i)-f^*(X_i))^2\le \frac{2}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n \varepsilon_i(\hat g_n(X_i)-f^*(X_i)),\]
then the same optimal rate as for the constrained least-squares estimator \(\hat f_n\) would follow. The dependence of such an inequality on the choice of \(S_n\) is not established in the paper.
problem_definition_latex:
Determine verifiable conditions on the error-tolerance sequence \((S_n)_{n\ge 1}\) (e.g., conditions expressed directly in terms of \(S_n\) and the data-generating model parameters such as \(\sigma^2\)) under which the estimator \(\hat g_n\) solving
\[\min_{f\in\mathcal{F}_m} J(f)\quad\text{subject to}\quad E_n(f)\le S_n\]
(i) is consistent for \(f^*\) and its weak partial derivatives of orders \(|\alpha|=1,\dots,m-1\), and (ii) admits a convergence rate characterization (in particular, a rate comparable to the optimal \(n^{-m/(2m+d)}\) rate achieved for the analogous smoothness-constrained least-squares estimator) in terms of \(n\), \(m\), \(d\), and the behavior of \(S_n\).
latex:
Let \(d\ge 1\) and \([a,b]^d\subset \mathbb{R}^d\) be a compact hyperrectangle. Let \(m\in\mathbb{N}\) satisfy \(2m>d\). Let \(\alpha=(\alpha_1,\dots,\alpha_d)\in\mathbb{Z}_+^d\) be a multi-index with order \(|\alpha|=\sum_{j=1}^d \alpha_j\), and let \(D^\alpha\) denote the (weak) partial derivative operator.

Let \(\mathcal{F}_m\) be the space of generalized functions (Schwartz distributions) on \(\mathbb{R}^d\) whose weak partial derivatives of order \(m\) are square-integrable, i.e.

Determine verifiable conditions on the error-tolerance sequence \((S_n)_{n\ge 1}\) (e.g., conditions expressed directly in terms of \(S_n\) and the data-generating model parameters such as \(\sigma^2\)) under which the estimator \(\hat g_n\) solving
\[\min_{f\in\mathcal{F}_m} J(f)\quad\text{subject to}\quad E_n(f)\le S_n\]
(i) is consistent for \(f^*\) and its weak partial derivatives of orders \(|\alpha|=1,\dots,m-1\), and (ii) admits a convergence rate characterization (in particular, a rate comparable to the optimal \(n^{-m/(2m+d)}\) rate achieved for the analogous smoothness-constrained least-squares estimator) in terms of \(n\), \(m\), \(d\), and the behavior of \(S_n\).
location_in_paper: Section 7.3 (page 15 of 27 in the provided text) and Remark 4 (page 10 of 27)
open_problem_quote: We conjecture that the conclusions of Corollary 2 hold under a more natural condition that \(S_n \to \sigma^2\) as \(n \to \infty\). This remains as an open question. Another open question is computing the convergence rate of \(\hat g_n\) under suitable conditions on \(S_n\).
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The statement is asymptotic and universal over function classes and data-generating distributions, so a finite automated search is unlikely to produce decisive counterexamples without substantial theoretical guidance on worst-case constructions and on how to discretize the infinite-dimensional function class.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can encode candidate rules for selecting \(S_n\) (and simulate data, solve the convex program for \(\hat g_n\), and estimate empirical risk/derivative error as a fitness signal). However, simulation-based fitness only partially reflects the required uniform-in-problem theoretical guarantees, and the convex-program solves may be computationally heavy for large-scale evolutionary search.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The open issue in Lima’s Problem (B) is to give verifiable conditions on the tolerance sequence \(S_n\) (ideally in terms of \(\sigma^2\)) that ensure the roughness-minimizing constrained estimator \(\hat g_n\) is consistent and achieves rates comparable to the Sobolev-optimal \(n^{-m/(2m+d)}\) rate established in the paper for the smoothness-constrained least-squares estimator (Problem (A)). Classical spline theory (Schoenberg; Reinsch) and thin-plate spline/RKHS theory (Duchon) show that constrained and penalized formulations are closely linked via Lagrange multipliers and that solutions lie in finite-dimensional spline spaces, but these works do not directly provide stochastic calibration rules for \(S_n\) under random design.

Rate results for penalized smoothing splines (e.g., Utreras and related work) and general empirical-process analyses of constrained/penalized regression over Sobolev balls (van de Geer) suggest a pathway: if \(S_n\) is chosen so that the constraint is feasible for \(f^*\) with high probability (typically \(S_n\ge E_n(f^*)\approx \sigma^2\) plus a deviation term of order \(n^{-1/2}\) under sub-Gaussian noise), then \(\hat g_n\) should behave like the penalized estimator with a data-dependent multiplier \(\lambda_n\), and one can aim to prove oracle inequalities translating control of \(E_n(\hat g_n)\) into control of \(\|\hat g_n-f^*\|_{L_2(P_X)}\) and derivative risks. However, a complete, sharp characterization of the optimal rate for \(\hat g_n\) as a function of the slack \(S_n-\sigma^2\) (and conditions ensuring the key inequality highlighted by Lima) does not appear to be settled in the provided materials, so the problem remains open.

sources.title: Spline functions and the problem of graduation
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces variational characterization of smoothing splines: minimizers of a roughness functional subject to interpolation/fit constraints, and establishes representer-type structure in 1D.
sources.connection: Provides foundational variational/representer framework for problems of the form minimize roughness subject to data-fit constraints, which underlies Problem (B) though not its stochastic rate analysis.

sources.title: Splines minimizing rotation-invariant semi-norms in Sobolev spaces
sources.theorem_or_result: Establishes existence and explicit representer for thin-plate splines as minimizers of Sobolev semi-norms with data-fit terms/constraints in m>d/2 settings.
sources.connection: Gives the RKHS/semi-Hilbert space structure of m>d/2 Sobolev spaces used in Lima’s \(\mathcal F_m\) and justifies that solutions to constrained/penalized problems live in finite-dimensional spline spaces, enabling analysis of \(\hat g_n\) via finite-dimensional convex programs.

sources.title: Smoothing by spline functions
sources.theorem_or_result: Studies smoothing splines and the relationship between constrained formulations (fit constraint vs roughness constraint) and penalized formulations in 1D; provides existence/uniqueness and computational insights.
sources.connection: Directly related to Problem (B) as an early analysis of minimizing roughness subject to a residual sum-of-squares bound, but primarily in 1D and without modern stochastic rates for random design.

sources.title: Convergence rates for spline smoothing
sources.theorem_or_result: Derives convergence rates for smoothing spline estimators (penalized least squares) under Sobolev smoothness assumptions.
sources.connection: Provides rate techniques for penalized formulation (Problem (C)) that can be transferred to constrained formulations via Lagrange multiplier equivalence, informing what rates are plausible for \(\hat g_n\) when \(S_n\) is calibrated.

sources.title: Estimating a regression function
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops empirical process tools and rates for nonparametric regression with complexity penalties/constraints, including Sobolev-type function classes.
sources.connection: Supplies general machinery (entropy bounds, oracle inequalities) that can be adapted to analyze constrained estimators like Problem (A)/(B), especially for random design and sub-Gaussian noise assumptions used by Lima.
